The subsidiary selects the best location based on availability and fulfilment capability. A Purchase Order is created and transmitted to the selected HQ via the Two-Tier ERP integration (2EJ). The chosen location fulfils and ships the order.

Value: Introduces intelligent, real-time multi-source procurement — the subsidiary makes the optimal sourcing decision without manual coordination between systems or teams.

Conclusion

The Two-Tier Cross ATP Check is infused with Joule AI capabilities through custom Joule skills, bringing cross-tier inventory intelligence directly into a single conversation. A subsidiary sales representative can check HQ availability, commit to a delivery date, create the sales order, and trigger the inter-company purchase order — all without switching systems. What once required manual coordination across two tiers now happens in one Joule interaction.

 

Stay Tuned for more details in Show and Tell session for Two-Tier ERP on 15th July 3.45pm IST. 

Note : This capability is custom Joule skill built on Joule Studio and is not delivered as part of standard product.

entry.title: 使用API创建交货单库存不足提醒
entry.content:
业务需求

客户在使用API创建外向交货单时，当库存不足小于销售订单数量时，API会直接生成外向交货单并取库存数量，这样会导致客户外围系统与S/4数据不一致，并且会无效占据库存。

系统实现

案例销售订单，行项目产品ZTG12数量100

库存数量剩下90

方法1

使用BADI：LE_SHP_SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE (Modify Standard and Custom Fields for Delivery Header and Items) 逻辑当库存数量小于销售订单行项目数量时，自定义字段赋值提醒：该产品库存不足

1.创建自定义字段YY1_Message

2.创建自定义逻辑并且发布

IF DOCUMENTPROCESSINGMODE = 'CREATE'.
CONSTANTS:
  lc_plant  TYPE werks_d VALUE '1310',
  lc_vkorg  TYPE vkorg   VALUE '1310',
  lc_vtweg  TYPE vtweg   VALUE '20',
  lc_spart  TYPE spart   VALUE '00'.

DATA:
  lv_so_qty TYPE menge_d,
  lv_stock TYPE menge_d.

move-corresponding delivery_document_in to delivery_document_out.
delivery_document_items_out = delivery_document_items_in.
LOOP AT delivery_document_items_in INTO DATA(ls_item).

  SELECT SINGLE orderquantity
    FROM i_salesorderitem
    WHERE salesorder     = @LS_item-referencesddocument
      AND salesorderitem = @LS_item-referencesddocumentitem
    INTO @LV_so_qty.
  " 读取物料的库存数量
  SELECT SINGLE MATLWRHSSTKQTYINMATLBASEUNIT
    FROM I_STOCKQUANTITYCURRENTVALUE_2( P_DisplayCurrency = 'CNY' )
    WHERE PRODUCT = @LS_item-MATERIAL
    INTO @LV_stock.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    CONTINUE. " 未找到对应销售订单行项目，跳过
  ENDIF.

  " 比较库存数量与销售订单数量
  IF lv_stock < lv_so_qty.
    " 更新自定义字段
    DELIVERY_DOCUMENT_OUT-yy1_message_dlh = 'Warning: 该物料库存数量不足。'.
  ENDIF.

ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.

3.创建外向交货单，系统会自动读取库存数量

保存后，查看交货单抬头自定义字段：

方法2

使用BADI：LE_SHP_DELIVERY_FINAL_CHECK (Last Checks Before Saving the Delivery)

逻辑当库存数量小于销售订单行项目数量时，系统报Error，这里不能设置报Warning，因为API创建时，会直接跳过Warning无视BADI。

 CONSTANTS:
    lc_plant    TYPE werks_d VALUE '1310',
    lc_vkorg    TYPE vkorg   VALUE '1310',
    lc_vtweg    TYPE vtweg   VALUE '20',
    lc_spart    TYPE spart   VALUE '00'.

    DATA:
    lv_so_qty    TYPE menge_d,
    lv_stock TYPE menge_d.

    IF documentprocessingmode = 'CREATE'.
      LOOP AT delivery_document_items_in INTO DATA(ls_item) .
      IF ls_item-plant = lc_plant.
      " 读取销售订单行项目的订单数量
      SELECT SINGLE orderquantity
      FROM i_salesorderitem
      WHERE salesorder     = @LS_item-referencesddocument
        AND salesorderitem = @LS_item-referencesddocumentitem
      INTO @LV_so_qty.
      " 读取物料的库存数量
      SELECT SINGLE MATLWRHSSTKQTYINMATLBASEUNIT
      FROM I_STOCKQUANTITYCURRENTVALUE_2( P_DisplayCurrency = 'CNY' )
      WHERE PRODUCT = @LS_item-MATERIAL
      INTO @LV_stock.
      IF sy-subrc <> 0.
        CONTINUE. " No SO item found – skip this delivery item
      ENDIF.
      IF lv_stock < lv_so_qty.
        message-messagetype = 'W'. " saving of the delivery document will be warned
        message-messagetext = |Warning: 该物料库存数量不足|.
        EXIT.
      ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

 

创建交货单

感谢观看

entry.title: AI-Assisted Cross-Tier Flexible Constraint Visibility in Two-Tier ERP
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's interconnected enterprise landscape, organizations often operate multiple ERP systems across headquarters, regional hubs, and subsidiaries. While each business entity plans independently, supply constraints are frequently managed centrally. This creates a challenge for planners who need visibility into available supply capacity from upstream organizations before making planning decisions.

To address this challenge, we developed an AI-Assisted Cross-Tier Flexible Constraint Visibility solution using Joule. The solution enables production planners in a subsidiary ERP system (Tier 2) to seamlessly query flexible constraints maintained in the headquarters ERP system (Tier 1) using natural language.

By leveraging SAP APIs and Joule's conversational capabilities, planners can instantly understand:

  • Maximum quantity available from headquarters
  • Flexible constraint limits for a material or a Production line
  • Available supply periods with the constraint quantity

This eliminates manual coordination between planning teams and enables faster, data-driven planning decisions.

Business Requirement

Organizations operating in a two-tier ERP landscape often face challenges in coordinating supply and production planning across headquarters which supplies subassemblies and subsidiaries which consumes subassemblies from HQ and manufactures finished product. Headquarters manages production line constraints and material supply constraints of the subassembly which will be considered for Mid-term/long-term planning using pMRP (Predictive Material and Resource Planning) simulation process, while subsidiary planners require timely visibility into these constraints for accurate planning of the finished product. In many cases, obtaining this information involves manual co-ordination, leading to delays, inefficiencies, and potential planning inaccuracies.

To address this challenge, the Joule-powered AI-Assisted Cross-Tier Flexible Constraint Visibility solution provides real-time access to headquarters-managed flexible constraints of subassembly directly within the subsidiary planning environment via joule, enabling more connected and informed planning decisions across

Two-Tier solution with Business Use-case

Consider a global manufacturing company where the headquarters operates on SAP Cloud ERP Private headquartered at European country produces the subassembly compressor and its subsidiary in APJ region operates on SAP Cloud ERP consumes compressor subassembly to manufacture the finished product (let’s say refrigerator). The headquarters maintains flexible constraints of the subassembly that define the maximum quantities available for supply across different planning periods. A production planner in a subsidiary needs to determine how much of a particular material can be supplied from headquarters before finalizing production plan for the finished product. Traditionally, this information would require coordination with headquarters planners, resulting in delays and manual effort.

With AI-Assisted Cross-Tier Flexible Constraint Visibility, the planner at subsidiary can simply ask Joule a natural language query such as,

"What is the maximum quantity headquarters can supply for Material Compressor?”

Joule retrieves the relevant flexible constraint information of the given material from the headquarters system and displays the available quantities with planning periods directly within the subsidiary environment via joule. This enables faster decision-making, improved collaboration between planning teams, and a more connected planning experience across organizational tiers.

Process Flow

Tier 1 – Headquarters (SAP Cloud ERP Private)

  • Supply planners at Tier1 define and maintain Flexible Constraints in SAP Cloud ERP Private.
  • Products or materials are assigned to the relevant flexible constraints.
  • Constraint periods are maintained with available quantities for each planning bucket (weekly, monthly, etc.).

Tier 2 – Subsidiary (SAP Cloud ERP)

  • A production planner at Tier2 initiates a query through Joule.
  • Joule interprets the planner's query and identifies the requested data.
  • Joule retrieves Flexible constraint details, product assignments, and available quantities in real time.
  • Joule consolidates and formats the information into a business-friendly response.
  • The planner at Tier 2 leverages the retrieved supply information in pMRP simulations for mid- and long-term planning of finished products, with the simulation results driving operational planning decisions.

Integration Details

The AI-Assisted Cross-Tier Flexible Constraint Visibility solution leverages standard SAP APIs exposed from the headquarters system (SAP Cloud ERP Private) and orchestrated through Joule. Depending on the planner's request, Joule dynamically invokes the relevant APIs to retrieve flexible constraint information and present it in a business-friendly format within the subsidiary environment.

The integration follows a request-response pattern where Joule acts as the conversational interface, the SAP APIs act as the source of truth for flexible constraint information, and the headquarters ERP system serves as the repository of supply commitments and constraint definitions.

This scenario is triggered when a production planner requests flexible constraint information for a specific material. For example:

"What is the maximum quantity headquarters can supply for Material Compressor in plant 1811 in the next quarter?"

Upon receiving the request, Joule identifies the material and invokes the relevant APIs in the headquarters system. The Flexible Constraint API retrieves the constraint header information, while the Flexible Constraint Period API retrieves the available quantities maintained for each planning period. Joule then consolidates the information and presents the available supply quantities to the planner.

APIs Used

API

Purpose

System

Flexible Constraint

Read flexible constraint header data

SAP Cloud ERP Private

Flexible Constraint Period

Read flexible constraint period and quantity

SAP Cloud ERP Private

Joule Orchestration Logic

Joule serves as the intelligent orchestration layer between the subsidiary planner and the headquarters ERP system. Based on the user's natural language request, Joule automatically invokes the appropriate APIs. The retrieved information is aggregated, interpreted, and presented in a conversational format, eliminating the need for planners to navigate technical applications or manually coordinate with headquarters.

This AI-driven approach provides real-time access to headquarters-managed supply constraints, enabling faster decision-making and more effective collaboration across organizational tiers.

Joule Demo:

To get visibility on the Flexible Constraint Header Data

The screenshot below demonstrates the cross-tier visibility capability, where Flexible Constraint IDs 1402 and 1403, already maintained in the Headquarters system, are retrieved and displayed in real time within the Subsidiary system through Joule.

To get visibility on the flexible constraint quantity for the corresponding period

Business Benefits

  • Real-time cross-tier visibility
  • Faster planning cycles and decision-making
  • Improved supply commitment accuracy
  • Enhanced collaboration across business units
  • AI-driven user experience through Joule

Conclusion

AI-Assisted Cross-Tier Flexible Constraint Visibility demonstrates how Joule can bridge information silos across enterprise planning systems. By enabling production planners in subsidiary organizations to access headquarters constraint information through natural language interactions, organizations can significantly improve planning efficiency and collaboration.

By enabling real-time visibility into headquarters-managed supply constraints, the solution helps create a seamless planning experience where critical supply information is available instantly, regardless of organizational boundaries.

Hope this blog gives you insights on getting cross-tier visibility on flexible constraints using joule.

Happy Learning!! 🙂

entry.title: 4-Eyes by Configuration: Multi-Processor Workflow Approvals in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
Introduction

A quick note before diving in: this blog was initially drafted with the help of SAP Joule for Consultants. — a great example of how AI tools can meaningfully accelerate the writing process.  And improved with Claude Sonnet. That said, all content, accuracy, and opinions here are entirely my own responsibility.

A special thank you to the colleagues who built and shipped these features under significant time pressure. Great software doesn’t happen without great teamwork — and it ultimately takes dedicated engineers to turn ideas into delivered, production-ready capabilities.

In today’s compliance-driven enterprise landscape, ensuring that critical business decisions are never made unilaterally is both a governance imperative and, in many industries, a regulatory requirement. Workflow automation has long been a cornerstone of SAP’s approach to streamlining approvals — but automation without embedded controls can introduce new risks rather than eliminate them.

Two foundational control mechanisms address this challenge directly:

  • Multi-Processor Task Completion — ensuring that a defined number of independent individuals must each process a task before a workflow can continue
  • Approver/Initiator Exclusion — ensuring that the person who approved or initiated a business document cannot also approve it, guaranteeing a second independent review (for exclusion of the initiator of a business document, see the linked resource)

With SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 SAP has introduced both of these capabilities natively within the Manage Workflow application and the broader flexible workflow framework — empowering Business Process Experts to configure robust dual-control and multi-control approval processes without writing a single line of code.

But governance is only as strong as its weakest configuration. That is why this release also introduces a new Validation Callback method that prevents misconfigured workflow definitions from ever being activated — closing the gap between documentation-based guidance and system-enforced compliance.

This blog covers all three features comprehensively: what they are, why they were introduced, how they work, and how to configure them correctly.

Background & Motivation

The Governance Gap in Workflow Automation

Prior to these enhancements, the Manage Workflows application offered two task completion modes for workflow step recipients:

Mode Runtime Behavior Limitation

One recipientA single shared task instance is created; the first person to act completes the stepNo guarantee of independent review
All recipientsA separate task instance is created for each recipient; every recipient must complete their taskInflexible — requires full group participation with no middle ground

Neither mode offered the precision required for a true four-eyes or six-eyes principle. Organizations needing exactly two or three independent completions had to rely on the "run with previous"  feature combined with manual process discipline or documentation-based guidance — all of which are fragile controls that do not scale in enterprise environments.

Multi-Processor Task Completion (4-Eyes / 6-Eyes via Configuration)

What Is It?

The Multi-Processor Task Completion feature addresses this gap directly: it ensures that a defined number of independent individuals each complete their own task instance before the workflow continues.

This is the mechanism that enables a true four-eyes principle (2 processors) or six-eyes principle (3 processors) in scenarios where multiple parallel approvers must each independently act — not just one of them, and not necessarily all of them.

Task Instance Behavior: The Three Modes

Completion mode is configured in the Recipients section of a workflow step using a radio button group:

Mode Runtime Behavior

One recipientA single shared task instance is created; the first recipient to act completes the step
Individual task per recipient — AllA multi-instance task is created; all recipients must complete the task
Two or three recipientsA multi-instance task is created; the defined number of recipients must approve, or one must reject

The two or three recipients option is the key addition in release 2608. It fills the gap between "one person acts" and "everyone must act" — enabling precise control over how many independent completions are required before the workflow proceeds.

Recipient Assignment Types

The recipient configuration UI is used consistently across steps, notifications, and other workflow artifacts. Supported assignment types include:

  • Agent Rule — dynamically resolves recipients based on business rules
  • Team — assigns the task to a predefined team (configured via Manage Teams and Responsibilities)
  • Concrete User — directly specifies one or more named users

Note: If you select the two or three recipients mode but assign only one concrete user, the system will display a validation error.

 Runtime Design

Several new UI elements have been introduced to support transparency at runtime:

  • A status bar at the task instance level shows who is currently processing a task within a team — and clearly indicates who has already approved.

 

  • The workflow instance component now includes an improved status bar displaying the number of required processors versus the number who have already acted.

     These additions give both approvers and process administrators real-time visibility into the state of multi-processor steps.

The Uniqueness Rule: One Person, One Completion

A critical safeguard of this feature is that the same person cannot complete more than one parallel task instance. This is what makes the four-eyes and six-eyes principles meaningful — it is not sufficient for two task instances to exist; they must be completed by two different people.

This uniqueness rule applies even in edge cases involving substitution and forwarding:

Scenario Behavior

SubstitutionIf Person A completes a task as a substitute for Person B, Person A cannot also complete their own parallel task instance
ForwardingIf Person A forwards their task to Person C, and Person C already has their own parallel instance, the uniqueness rule still applies

Resolution for blocked users: If a person is unable to complete their own task because the uniqueness rule applies (e.g., they already acted via substitution on a parallel instance), they can — where permitted — forward their own task instance to another eligible person. This self-service resolution requires no administrative intervention.

An explicit error message is displayed when a user attempts to complete a second task instance in violation of this rule.

Validation Callback for Mandatory Step Configurations

The Problem It Solves

The introduction of the N-processor option increases the risk that a workflow modeler will inadvertently misconfigure a step that must always be set to "All of the recipients." The "Verify Intercompany General Journal Entry" step is the primary example — but the pattern applies to any scenario where a specific completion mode is a non-negotiable compliance requirement.

Previously, this requirement was enforced only through documentation. With the 2608 release, a validation callback mechanism now prevents the activation of a workflow definition if a mandatory step is incorrectly configured — catching errors before they affect live business processes.

How the Validation Works

The validation is implemented via the if_swf_flex_ifs_def_appl~on_validation_callback method. When a workflow modeler attempts to activate a workflow definition, the system executes this callback. If the VerifyMultiInstance step is not configured to require all processors (cv_all_processors), the system raises a validation exception and blocks activation.

This represents a significant improvement over documentation-only guidance:

  • It operates at activation time, not at runtime — catching errors before they affect live processes
  • It is automatic and non-bypassable — a modeler cannot accidentally skip the validation
  • It provides a clear error message directing the modeler to correct the configuration
  • It scales — the same pattern can be applied to any scenario where specific configurations are mandatory

Code Reference (Baseline for Implementation)

The following ABAP snippet serves as a baseline for implementing the validation callback:

METHOD if_swf_flex_ifs_def_appl~on_validation_callback.  
DATA(lt_activity) = io_workflow->get_all_activities( ).  
" Raise a validation error if the multi-instance step is not configured to require all processors.  
LOOP AT lt_activity INTO DATA(lo_activity).    
IF lo_activity->get_step_id( ) = 'VerifyMultiInstance'.      
DATA(lv_number_of_proc) = lo_activity->get_number_of_requ_processors( ).      
IF lv_number_of_proc <> lo_activity->cv_all_processors.        
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_swf_flex_ifs_exception          
MESSAGE ID 'Your Message Class'
TYPE 'E'          
NUMBER 'Your Message Number'.      
ENDIF.    
ENDIF.  
ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Scope and Intentional Boundaries

It is important to understand the deliberate scope boundaries of this feature:

In Scope Out of Scope

Validation at activation time that prevents an invalid workflow definition from being activatedA UX-level change that hides or disables invalid configuration options in the UI (e.g., greying out the "2 processors" option for the VerifyMultiInstance step)

Discussions are already underway to explore UX improvements that would disable invalid options directly in the configuration interface.

Combined Use & Real-World Scenarios

When to Use Which Feature

Understanding when to apply each feature — and how they complement each other — is key to designing effective compliance-oriented workflows:

Requirement Recommended Feature

Prevent the initiator from approving their own requestExcluded Agents
Require exactly 2 independent people to each complete a taskTwo recipients mode
Require exactly 3 independent people to each complete a taskThree recipients mode
Require all members of a group to complete a taskAll recipients mode
Prevent a specific step from being misconfigured at activationValidation Callback
Combine initiator exclusion with multi-processor completion4-Eyes Principle + Multi-Processor (both active simultaneously)

In many real-world scenarios, all three features will be active simultaneously — delivering layered, system-enforced governance across the full approval lifecycle.

Example: Financial Processes — Journal Entry Postings

For multi-level verification scenarios — such as a 4-level approval hierarchy based on distinct monetary thresholds across multiple company codes — workflows can be defined with sequentially ordered steps, each with its own amount-based precondition and assigned approver group. The multi-processor task completion feature ensures that each level of that hierarchy meets the required independent review count.

Configuration Checklist & Getting Started

Prerequisites and System Requirements

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 or higher
  • Flexible Workflow must be active and configured for the relevant business object
  • Users must be set up as business users with appropriate authorizations
  • The Manage Teams and Responsibilities application is recommended for agent determination
  • For scenario developers: the Validation Callback is implemented via the on_validation_callback method

Best Practices

  • Always test your workflow configuration using the Simulation feature (available from SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408 onwards) — verify which workflow definition triggers for a given business object instance before going live
  • Explicitly configure excluded agents to avoid workflow stalls or compliance gaps when the initiator is the only eligible approver
  • Define clear preconditions for each step in multi-step scenarios to avoid unintended overlaps in approval routing

entry.title: Decentralized Extended Warehouse Management and Quality Management in Two-Tier ERP
entry.content:
The SAP Community blog by @Sahil_Grover introduces a hybrid integration between decentralized SAP EWM and SAP Cloud ERP.

  • SAP EWM Integration - Inbound Processing from Supplier & Outbound Processing to Customer

The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 1

  • Setup for The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP

The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 2

In this blog, we introduce a new scenario for SAP Decentralized EWM integration with Quality Management, where Quality Inspection is executed directly in EWM using the Quality Inspection Engine (QIE).

This approach is especially relevant for businesses that want to perform inspection decisions closer to warehouse execution, reduce dependency on ERP-based inspection activities, and enable faster Inbound processing.

Business Scenario Overview

In this scenario:

  • A Purchase order and Inbound delivery are created in SAP Cloud ERP
  • The Inbound delivery is distributed to the Decentralized EWM
  • Goods receipt is posted in EWM
  • Quality inspection is triggered and executed directly in EWM
  • Inspection results and follow-up actions are performed within EWM
  • Stock status is updated and synchronized with SAP Cloud ERP

The key differentiator here is that inspection happens exactly where the goods are physically handled — in the warehouse.

Key Configuration Focus Areas

To enable this scenario successfully, some important setup areas need attention.

  1. Quality Inspection Engine (QIE) in EWM

The Inspection object type, Inspection rules, and decision logic need to be configured correctly so that Inspection documents are created and processed as expected.

       2. Product and Warehouse Master Data

The warehouse product master and material setup must support inspection-relevant processing. Stock determination and warehouse execution behavior should align with the quality process.

       3. Delivery Integration Between SAP Cloud ERP and EWM

Inbound delivery distribution must work correctly so that the warehouse receives the required execution documents in time.

       4. Follow-Up Action Determination

Accepted, Rejected, and Exception outcomes should be mapped carefully to the right stock types and warehouse actions.

       5. Warehouse Process Integration

Inspection may be required at goods receipt, before putaway, at handling unit level, or in work center-related steps depending on the business design.

A well-designed configuration ensures that warehouse execution remains smooth and the quality process behaves consistently.

Process Flow

  1. Purchase Order and Inbound Delivery in SAP Cloud ERP

The process begins in ERP with procurement. Once the inbound delivery is created, it is distributed to the decentralized EWM system for warehouse execution.

       2. Goods Receipt in in Decentralized EWM

When the goods arrive physically, the warehouse posts goods receipt in EWM. At this point, inspection-relevant checks are triggered.

        3. Inspection Document Creation via QIE

Based on configured rules, EWM creates an inspection document using the Quality Inspection Engine. This document becomes the operational object for the warehouse-level inspection.

        4. Inspection Execution in EWM

The inspection is performed directly in EWM by the warehouse or quality user. This may include visual checks, packaging verification, quantity checks, damage checks, or handling-unit-level validation.

The warehouse user records the inspection result in EWM and completes the inspection process without switching to ERP for operational execution.

This is particularly useful in decentralized warehouse scenarios where speed and execution simplicity are critical.

       5. Usage Decision / Inspection Outcome in EWM

After inspection execution, the user records the final inspection outcome in EWM.

Typical outcomes include: Accepted, Rejected, Partially accepted, Rework required, Return to vendor, and Scrap

        6. Follow-Up Action in EWM

After the inspection result is recorded, EWM automatically determines the next action. Stock can be released to Unrestricted use, Blocked, routed for rework, returned to Supplier, or scrapped based on the configured decision logic

Conclusion

The 7L1-based Two-Tier Decentralized EWM scenario with EWM-based Quality Inspection is a strong option for businesses that want inspection to happen closer to warehouse execution.

By using QIE in EWM, organizations can inspect inbound goods directly in the warehouse, record decisions immediately, and trigger the right follow-up action without delaying operational flow.

In a Two-Tier SAP architecture, this approach provides a practical balance between central ERP control and local warehouse execution efficiency.

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: From reactive maintenance to autonomous agents
entry.content:
Unplanned equipment downtime can cost industrial companies tens or even hundreds of thousands of dollars per hour. In highly asset-intensive industries, losses can be even higher. This is not just a maintenance issue. It is a business issue that touches revenue, safety, compliance, and brand reputation.

Every business that depends on physical equipment, machines in a factory, turbines in a power plant, infrastructure in a warehouse, faces a fundamental question: how do you keep your operations running reliably without overspending on maintenance or exposing your workforce to unnecessary risk?

Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (SAP Cloud ERP) addresses exactly this challenge. It is a core ERP capability, built on decades of SAP expertise across asset-intensive industries, that goes far beyond a simple maintenance tool. In this post, I share insights from a recent conversation with Akif Ünal (Product Management) and Amar Kumar (Product Marketing) at SAP, where we explored what SAP delivers today, how real customers are transforming their operations, and what the future looks like with AI-powered agents.

Watch the full Expert Talk: 

Why asset management matters more than you think

Consider a simple analogy: owning a car. When you buy a car, your journey does not end at the dealership, it begins there. You perform regular oil changes, check tire pressure, listen for unusual sounds. You do all of this to ensure reliability, maintain performance, and protect the long-term value of that asset.

Now scale that up. Your company does not have a single car. It has a fleet of critical, high-value equipment: production lines, heavy machinery, essential infrastructure. Not managing them properly is not just an inconvenience, it directly impacts the bottom line.

The business case for asset management rests on three pillars:

Preventing unplanned breakdowns. When a critical machine fails unexpectedly, production stops, customer orders are delayed, and revenue is lost. Effective asset management moves you from costly reactive firefighting to planned, proactive maintenance, predicting and preventing failures before they happen.

Controlling total cost of ownership. Like with a car, the purchase price is only part of the story. Asset management provides the visibility to understand true maintenance costs, analyze spending patterns on recurring versus breakdown maintenance, and simulate future costs to create smarter budgets.

Making your teams faster and smarter. Today, planners and supervisors often navigate multiple systems to get a complete picture of an asset’s history and health. Modern asset management consolidates all that data into a single, intelligent view, empowering teams to identify recurring problems, optimize schedules, and make confident decisions.

For a company already running SAP, this is not about adding another system. They have now the possibility to add value of the critical assets you already own and turning what is often seen as a cost center into a driver of operational excellence.

What SAP Cloud ERP actually covers

Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is not merely a maintenance application. It is an integrated end-to-end solution embedded directly into the operational and financial backbone of the enterprise. Built on standardized master data, technical object structures, reusable configuration, and proven best-practice processes, SAP enables comprehensive asset management out of the box.

 

Asset Management capabilities overview in SAP Cloud ERP

Operationally, SAP supports the full spectrum of maintenance strategies:

Reactive maintenance handles breakdowns, emergency repairs, and fast-track corrective actions when failures occur. It is the safety net when something goes wrong despite your best efforts.

Proactive and preventive maintenance helps companies avoid failures altogether through planned strategies based on time intervals, operation hours, counters, measurement points, and condition monitoring.

Improvement maintenance covers larger engineering-driven activities such as plant upgrades, redesigns, and operational optimization initiatives—coordinated through the project system that controls all activities, tasks, and costs.

Operations and overhead maintenance addresses activities that are not part of typical maintenance tasks, such as training, documentation, and development work.

A key differentiator is the deep ERP integration. Asset management processes are directly connected with logistics, procurement, inventory management, and workforce-related processes in one harmonized cloud environment. This tight integration eliminates the silos that traditionally make asset management fragmented and expensive to maintain.

Real-world transformation: A gas company in the Middle East

To understand the tangible impact, consider a gas company in the Middle East—a joint venture of a global gas company giant operating in a challenging environment. Before their transformation, they ran a heavily customized legacy ERP system with custom-built solutions for asset management.

The pain was significant: their asset management solution was not integrated with the rest of the ERP processes. It was disconnected from logistics, procurement, and finance. They had built extensive custom integrations that brought higher operational costs, prevented them from staying current with SAP standards, and left them without access to modern functionality.

When they moved to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition the transformation was immediate and measurable. The modern Fiori-first user experience meant that every transaction was accessible through a simple web browser, a significant improvement for field teams. Standardized best-practice processes for reactive, proactive, and all other maintenance types replaced their fragmented custom solutions.

Most importantly, the cloud platform delivered real-time insights across asset management, finance, procurement, and logistics in a single environment. And because they are now on the cloud, every six months SAP delivers the latest innovations automatically. They no longer have to plan and execute expensive upgrade projects to benefit from new capabilities.

This example illustrates a pattern we see across industries: moving to a standardized cloud platform with integrated asset management brings not only operational efficiency but positions the organization for continuous, future-proof innovation.

The AI vision: From conversational AI to autonomous agents

While Joule provides a strong conversational entry point for AI in asset management, SAP’s vision is much larger. The goal is to fundamentally change how key personas in asset management work every day, moving toward what SAP calls the autonomous enterprise.

Consider the challenges these personas face today:

The maintenance supervisor is responsible for screening notifications and deciding which ones to accept or reject. In a world of connected equipment and IoT platforms, every anomaly raises a notification. When thousands of notifications arrive for the same issue, the triage work becomes overwhelming. AI can help by intelligently grouping, deduplicating, and prioritizing these notifications.

The maintenance planner faces even more complexity. They must coordinate in-house and external technicians, review maintenance history, control costs under constant pressure, and navigate multiple applications for the information they need. AI can synthesize fragmented data into a single on-demand summary of the asset and recommend optimal planning decisions.

The maintenance technician works in the field and needs the simplest possible way to report issues. Instead of manually creating notifications, a technician can take a photograph and ask the AI agent to create a notification of a defect, reducing administrative burden and speeding up the entire response chain.

The Operational Maintenance Planner Agent

The Operational Maintenance Planner Agent: from notification to execution-ready work order in minutes

One of the most compelling examples of this AI vision in action is the Operational Maintenance Planner Agent. This agent generates complete work order structures from approved notifications, recommending order types, operations, task lists, work centers, and durations based on context and maintenance history.

In practice, this works through a conversational interface. The planner asks the agent which notifications are ready for planning. The agent retrieves the list, provides a comprehensive summary of each notification, and offers options: search for reference orders from the past, or create a new work order. If the planner chooses to look at history, the system searches business data, both master data and transactional records, and returns matching reference orders with confidence scores.

The result: what previously required navigating four or five different applications and manually correlating information now happens in minutes through a natural, conversational flow. The human remains in the loop at every decision point, but the heavy lifting of data gathering, correlation, and recommendation is handled by the agent.

The agent also executes parallel readiness checks across all prerequisites, initiates required processes (such as work permits), and communicates with other agents for planning optimization. The outcome is a significant reduction in mean time to repair.

Looking ahead: The roadmap

AI Agent roadmap for Asset Management

The Operational Maintenance Planner Agent is just the beginning. SAP has a comprehensive pipeline of AI agents planned for asset management:

In the second half of 2026, expect the Functional Location Insight Agent, the Maintenance Request Initiation Agent, the Maintenance Request Screening Agent, and the Maintenance Activity Insight Agent. Moving into 2027, the Equipment Insight Agent, the Repair or Replace Recommendation Agent, the Maintenance Job Debrief Agent, and the Work Permit Recommendation Agent are on the roadmap.

The takeaway

Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is not just a maintenance tool. It is a business resilience platform that protects your assets, protects your workers, and helps you control costs with greater precision.

With AI, the shift is clear: you stop reacting to failures and start predicting them. The agents coming in the next releases will transform how supervisors, planners, and technicians work, reducing manual effort, accelerating decision-making, and ensuring that the right maintenance happens at the right time.

If you are already running Asset Management, now is the time to explore capabilities you may not have activated yet. The platform is evolving rapidly, and every six months brings new functionality that is automatically available to you.

Want to dive deeper? Watch the full SAP Expert Talk session:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fyx68fY2be0

You can also reach us at insides4@sap.com for questions or to share your feedback.
